subject: [PATCH] mm/lruvec: trace LRU add drains and drain-all queuing

reviews.author: JP Kobryn (author)
reviews.summary: Author questioned the necessity of adding a dedicated tracepoint, suggesting that existing tracing mechanisms like fentry or kprobe could be used instead.
reviews.raw_body:
Right, but couldn't that already be done with fentry or kprobe? If we
only need the calling stack and the argument value of force_all_cpus I
don't see a strong need for a dedicated tracepoint.

reviews.author: JP Kobryn (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged the need to add a separate tracepoint for mm_lru_add_drain, agreed to restructure in version 3 of the patch.
reviews.raw_body:
Nevermind that. I see it's declared inline so I'll add a tracepoint and
send v3.

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that kprobes and fentries may be inlined by the compiler, potentially making them unreliable for tracing purposes.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks. BTW even without inline keyword, compiler can still decide to inline
a function, so kprobe/fentry are not always reliable.

2026-06-10.patch_summary: This patch adds two new tracepoints to the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) subsystem: mm_lru_add_drain and mm_lru_drain_all_queue. The first tracepoint reports when an LRU add batch is drained, allowing tracing of partial drains and attribution to calling stacks. The second tracepoint captures when per-CPU drain work is queued for remote drain operations, including the requester stack and target CPU. These additions improve debugging capabilities by providing more detailed information about memory management events.
